problem.value: Staff need a fast, reliable way to verify attendee registration and admit them at the entrance, replacing manual list-checking and reducing entry fraud/duplicates.
problem.evidence: "staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day" — implies verification against a registration list, not stated explicitly as the underlying pain.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — unknown whether today it's paper lists, spreadsheets, or another ticketing tool.

success_metrics.value: Likely: check-in speed per attendee, zero duplicate/fraudulent entries, accurate real-time attendance count.
success_metrics.evidence: Implicit in the entrance check-in use case; not stated by client.

actors.value: Staff (check-in operators) and Attendees are named. Likely also an event organizer/admin for setup and a supervisor role for overrides.
actors.evidence: "staff to check attendees in" explicitly names staff and attendees.

business_objects.value: Event, Attendee/Registration, Ticket, Check-in record. Source of attendee data undetermined.
business_objects.evidence: Implied by "check attendees in" — an attendee list must exist somewhere.

business_rules.evidence: Not specified: what makes an attendee valid (ticket type, paid status, revoked/cancelled registration, one-per-person vs plus-ones, capacity caps).

workflow.evidence: No detail on check-in states (registered → checked-in → checked-out), multiple entry points, or re-entry handling.

integrations.evidence: Unclear whether attendee list comes from an external registration/ticketing system, and whether sync is real-time (online) or offline/batch.

permissions.value: Staff can scan/check-in; likely a supervisor role can override rejected entries or edit records.
permissions.evidence: Only "staff" mentioned as actor; no distinction of roles or override rights given.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Unclear if this check-in app must be reusable across multiple clients/events (configurable) or built for a single one-off event.

constraints.value: Venue entrances often have poor/no connectivity; device type (phone/tablet/scanner) and offline capability likely critical but unconfirmed.
constraints.evidence: Implicit in "venue entrance" context — a classic scenario for connectivity constraints, not stated by client.

edge_cases.value: Duplicate check-in attempts, lost/forgotten ticket, walk-in without registration, revoked ticket, simultaneous check-in at multiple gates.
edge_cases.evidence: Standard entry-control edge cases, not addressed in the request.

reporting.evidence: No mention, but real-time attendance count may be needed for venue capacity/safety compliance — raises this above the low baseline.

acceptance.evidence: Not defined — no criteria given for what "working" check-in looks like on event day.

risks.evidence: Not addressed: device failure, network outage, entry bottlenecks, staff training on event day itself (no re-run possible).

questions.q: Will the venue have reliable internet/network access, and must the app work fully offline (with later sync) if connectivity drops during the event?
questions.why: high impact × empty: this is the classic blind spot for entrance check-in apps — offline-capable vs. always-online is a fundamentally different architecture, not a tweak.

questions.q: What makes an attendee valid for entry — ticket type, payment status, a specific guest list, capacity limits, or revoked/cancelled registrations? Can one ticket admit multiple people (plus-ones)?
questions.why: high impact × empty: the validation logic drives the entire check-in flow and error handling; without it we'd guess the rules.

questions.q: Where does the attendee/registration list come from — an existing ticketing/registration system? Should check-ins sync back to it in real time, or is a same-day export/import enough?
questions.why: high impact × empty: determines whether this is a standalone tool or a live-synced module, and whether check-in is synchronous/blocking or eventual.

questions.q: Can any staff member check in anyone, or are there roles (e.g., a supervisor who can override a rejected/duplicate check-in, or staff restricted to a specific gate/list)?
questions.why: high impact × low completeness: entrance control without override rights forces staff to turn away legitimate attendees on the spot.

questions.q: Is this app being built for a single event, or must it be reusable/configurable across multiple future events and clients (different rules, branding, entry lists)?
questions.why: high impact × empty: changes whether we hardcode this event's rules or build a configurable platform feature.

questions.q: How is the attendee list managed today (spreadsheet, another ticketing tool, manual sign-in), and what does success look like on the day (e.g., avg. check-in time, zero duplicates, accurate headcount)?
questions.why: high impact × empty: without knowing the baseline, we can't size the effort or measure whether the app actually improves entry speed and accuracy.

summary.objective: Give venue staff a fast, reliable tool to verify and check in attendees at the entrance on event day, replacing manual list-checking and reducing fraudulent/duplicate entries.
summary.scope: Likely: a staff-facing app (mobile/tablet) to scan or look up attendees against a registration list, mark them as checked-in, prevent duplicate entries, and give a live headcount. Scope depends heavily on where the attendee list originates and whether the venue has reliable connectivity.
assumptions: An attendee/registration list already exists and needs to be checked against, rather than created from scratch in this app.
Staff need at least a basic override/supervisor capability for edge cases (lost ticket, disputed entry).
Real-time or same-day attendance count is valuable for organizers and possibly venue safety/capacity compliance.
Device/connectivity at the venue entrance may be unreliable, requiring offline-first design.
This is likely a single-event build first, with configurability for future events as a possible but unconfirmed extension.
summary.blind_spot: Offline capability: venue entrances are notorious for poor connectivity, and 'check attendees in' does not specify whether the app must function without network access. If it must, this is a fundamentally different build (local data store, conflict-free sync, queued writes) than a simple online lookup tool — and discovering this on event day, with no chance to fix it live, is the real risk.
